title: App Modularization & Build System
description: Module types, dependency graphs, version catalogs, convention plugins, KSP, multi-module architecture, Hilt in multi-module, and build optimization

references.title: Android Developers — Guide to App Modularization

lesson:
Why Modularize?

Modularization is the #1 differentiator at senior Android interviews. A well-modularized app has faster builds (parallel compilation), stricter encapsulation, independent feature teams, and reusable components.

Five reasons to modularize:
(1) Build speed — Gradle compiles independent modules in parallel. Change one feature? Only that module recompiles.
(2) Encapsulation — internal visibility is enforced per module. Implementation details can't leak.
(3) Team ownership — Team A owns :feature:home, Team B owns :feature:profile. No merge conflicts.
(4) Reusability — :core:ui (your design system) can be shared across apps or published as a library.
(5) Dynamic delivery — With Play Feature Delivery, feature modules can be downloaded on-demand, reducing initial APK size.

Multi-Module Architecture

A multi-module app is organized as a graph of Gradle modules, each with a specific responsibility. The most proven pattern (used by Now in Android, Google apps, and most production Android apps) splits modules into three categories: app, feature, and core.

Module Types

Module Type Diagram: :app THE APPLICATION MODULE | - Depends on ALL feature modules | - Contains Application class, NavHost, | main Activity, app-level config | - Wires features together via navigation | +-- :feature:home FEATURE MODULES (one per screen/flow) +-- :feature:profile - Self-contained: ViewModel, Screen, UiState +-- :feature:settings - Depends only on :core:* modules +-- :feature:search - NEVER depends on other :feature: modules | - Can be worked on independently by a team | +-- :core:data CORE MODULES (shared infrastructure) | | - Repository implementations | | - Data sources (Room + Retrofit) | | - Mappers (DTO -> Domain, Entity -> Domain) | | +-- :core:domain - Domain models (plain Kotlin, no Android deps) | - Repository interfaces | - Use cases (optional) | +-- :core:network - Retrofit setup, OkHttp client | - API service interfaces | - Network DTOs | +-- :core:database - Room database, DAOs, entities | - Migrations | +-- :core:ui - Shared composables (AppBar, Loading, Error) | - Theme (colors, typography, shapes) | - Design system components | +-- :core:common - Utility extensions | - Constants | - Result wrappers | +-- :core:testing - Test utilities, fakes, test rules - Shared test fixtures

Dependency Rules

The most important rule in multi-module architecture: features NEVER depend on other features. This is what makes parallel compilation, independent team ownership, and clean architecture possible.

Dependency Graph — Allowed vs Forbidden: :app / | \\ / | \\ v v v :feature: :feature: :feature: home profile settings | \\ | \\ | \\ | \\ | \\ | \\ v v v v v v :core:data :core:ui :core:domain :core:common | | | v v v :core:network :core:database (pure Kotlin) ALLOWED arrows (top to bottom): :app --> :feature:* (assembles all features) :feature:* --> :core:* (uses shared infrastructure) :core:data --> :core:network (data layer uses network) :core:data --> :core:database (data layer uses database) :core:data --> :core:domain (implements domain interfaces) FORBIDDEN arrows: :feature:home --> :feature:profile (features must be independent) :core:* --> :feature:* (core can't depend on features) :core:ui --> :core:data (UI doesn't know about data)
Why features must not depend on each other: If :feature:home depends on :feature:profile, then (1) changing profile forces home to recompile — killing build parallelism, (2) you can't work on profile without potentially breaking home, (3) extracting profile into a dynamic feature module becomes impossible, and (4) you're one step away from circular dependencies.

Module Types (Now in Android Pattern)

:app +-- :feature:home +-- :feature:search +-- :feature:profile | +-- :core:data (repositories, data sources) +-- :core:network (Retrofit, OkHttp setup) +-- :core:database (Room DAOs, entities) +-- :core:ui (shared composables, theme) +-- :core:model (domain models, no Android deps) +-- :core:common (utilities, extensions) +-- :core:testing (test utilities, fakes)
Key rule: Feature modules NEVER depend on each other. They only depend on :core:* modules. This prevents circular dependencies and enables parallel builds.

Gradle Configuration for Multi-Module

Version Catalogs (libs.versions.toml)

Centralize all dependency versions in gradle/libs.versions.toml. This is the modern replacement for ext blocks and buildSrc constants.

# gradle/libs.versions.toml [versions] kotlin = "2.0.0" compose-bom = "2024.06.00" retrofit = "2.11.0" room = "2.6.1" hilt = "2.51.1" coroutines = "1.8.1" agp = "8.5.0" [libraries] # AndroidX androidx-core-ktx = { group = "androidx.core", name = "core-ktx", version = "1.13.1" } androidx-lifecycle-runtime = { group = "androidx.lifecycle", name = "lifecycle-runtime-compose", version = "2.8.3" } # Compose (using BOM for version alignment) compose-bom = { group = "androidx.compose", name = "compose-bom", version.ref = "compose-bom" } compose-ui = { group = "androidx.compose.ui", name = "ui" } compose-material3 = { group = "androidx.compose.material3", name = "material3" } # Networking retrofit-core = { group = "com.squareup.retrofit2", name = "retrofit", version.ref = "retrofit" } retrofit-moshi = { group = "com.squareup.retrofit2", name = "converter-moshi", version.ref = "retrofit" } # Room room-runtime = { group = "androidx.room", name = "room-runtime", version.ref = "room" } room-compiler = { group = "androidx.room", name = "room-compiler", version.ref = "room" } room-ktx = { group = "androidx.room", name = "room-ktx", version.ref = "room" } # Hilt hilt-android = { group = "com.google.dagger", name = "hilt-android", version.ref = "hilt" } hilt-compiler = { group = "com.google.dagger", name = "hilt-android-compiler", version.ref = "hilt" } [bundles] compose = ["compose-ui", "compose-material3"] room = ["room-runtime", "room-ktx"] retrofit = ["retrofit-core", "retrofit-moshi"] [plugins] android-application = { id = "com.android.application", version.ref = "agp" } android-library = { id = "com.android.library", version.ref = "agp" } kotlin-android = { id = "org.jetbrains.kotlin.android", version.ref = "kotlin" } hilt = { id = "com.google.dagger.hilt.android", version.ref = "hilt" }

Then in any module: implementation(libs.retrofit.core) — type-safe, auto-completed by IDE.

Bundles let you group related libraries: implementation(libs.bundles.compose) adds both compose-ui and compose-material3 in one line. This reduces boilerplate and ensures related libs are always added together.

api vs implementation

Gradle dependency configurations control visibility and build speed:

ConfigurationBehaviorUse When
implementationDependency is internal — consumers can't see itDefault choice. Keeps build fast.
apiDependency is exposed — consumers can use it directlyOnly when your public API exposes types from the dependency
// :core:data/build.gradle.kts dependencies { implementation(libs.retrofit) // internal — feature modules can't see Retrofit types api(libs.kotlinx.coroutines.core) // exposed — features use Flow/coroutines from this api(project(":core:model")) // exposed — features use domain models }
Why this matters for build speed: With implementation, changing Retrofit's version doesn't recompile feature modules (they can't see Retrofit types). With api, ANY change to the dependency forces ALL consumers to recompile. Use api only when a dependency's types appear in your module's public interface (function signatures, return types, superclasses).
// Decision flowchart: api or implementation? // Does Retrofit appear in :core:data's PUBLIC functions? class UserRepositoryImpl( private val api: UserApi // private — not exposed ) : UserRepository { suspend fun getUsers(): List<User> // returns domain model, not Response<T> } // Answer: NO. Retrofit is an implementation detail → use implementation // Does :core:model appear in :core:data's PUBLIC functions? interface UserRepository { suspend fun getUsers(): List<User> // User is from :core:model } // Answer: YES. User is in the public API → use api

Convention Plugins — Sharing Build Config Across Modules

Instead of copy-pasting build config across 20 modules, define reusable plugins in build-logic/:

// build-logic/convention/src/main/kotlin/AndroidFeatureConventionPlugin.kt class AndroidFeatureConventionPlugin : Plugin<Project> { override fun apply(target: Project) { with(target) { pluginManager.apply("com.android.library") pluginManager.apply("org.jetbrains.kotlin.android") pluginManager.apply("dagger.hilt.android.plugin") extensions.configure<LibraryExtension> { compileSdk = 35 defaultConfig.minSdk = 26 buildFeatures.compose = true } dependencies { add("implementation", project(":core:ui")) add("implementation", project(":core:model")) add("implementation", project(":core:common")) add("implementation", libs.findLibrary("hilt.android").get()) add("ksp", libs.findLibrary("hilt.compiler").get()) } } } } // :feature:home/build.gradle.kts — ONE LINE instead of 30! plugins { id("myapp.android.feature") } dependencies { implementation(project(":core:data")) // That's it! Compose, Hilt, compileSdk, minSdk — all handled by convention plugin }
// build-logic/convention/src/main/kotlin/AndroidLibraryConventionPlugin.kt class AndroidLibraryConventionPlugin : Plugin<Project> { override fun apply(target: Project) { with(target) { pluginManager.apply("com.android.library") pluginManager.apply("org.jetbrains.kotlin.android") extensions.configure<LibraryExtension> { compileSdk = 35 defaultConfig.minSdk = 26 } } } } // :core:data/build.gradle.kts plugins { id("myapp.android.library") id("myapp.android.hilt") } dependencies { implementation(project(":core:network")) implementation(project(":core:database")) api(project(":core:model")) }
Convention plugin hierarchy: Create composable plugins — myapp.android.library (base), myapp.android.feature (adds Compose + Hilt + navigation), myapp.android.hilt (just Hilt setup). Feature plugin can apply library plugin internally.

Real build.gradle.kts Examples

// ======================================== // :feature:home/build.gradle.kts // ======================================== plugins { id("myapp.android.feature") // convention: library + kotlin + compose + hilt } dependencies { implementation(project(":core:data")) implementation(project(":core:domain")) testImplementation(project(":core:testing")) testImplementation(libs.junit) testImplementation(libs.turbine) testImplementation(libs.coroutines.test) } // ======================================== // :core:data/build.gradle.kts // ======================================== plugins { id("myapp.android.library") id("myapp.android.hilt") } dependencies { api(project(":core:domain")) // domain models in public API implementation(project(":core:network")) implementation(project(":core:database")) implementation(libs.bundles.retrofit) implementation(libs.bundles.room) implementation(libs.coroutines.core) } // ======================================== // :core:domain/build.gradle.kts // ======================================== plugins { id("org.jetbrains.kotlin.jvm") // pure Kotlin — no Android deps! Fastest to compile. } dependencies { implementation(libs.coroutines.core) // for Flow in repository interfaces implementation(libs.javax.inject) // for @Inject on use cases }
Make :core:domain a pure Kotlin module (not Android library). It has no Android dependencies — just data classes and interfaces. This makes it the fastest module to compile and keeps your domain layer truly framework-independent.

Hilt in Multi-Module Projects

Hilt works seamlessly across modules because of how Dagger's compile-time graph merging works. Each module contributes its own @Module classes, and Hilt aggregates them into the final component in the :app module.

Each Module Has Its Own @Module/@InstallIn

// :core:network — provides Retrofit and API services @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object NetworkModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideRetrofit(): Retrofit = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/") .addConverterFactory(MoshiConverterFactory.create()) .build() @Provides @Singleton fun provideUserApi(retrofit: Retrofit): UserApi = retrofit.create(UserApi::class.java) } // :core:database — provides Room database and DAOs @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object DatabaseModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideDatabase(@ApplicationContext ctx: Context): AppDatabase = Room.databaseBuilder(ctx, AppDatabase::class.java, "app.db").build() @Provides fun provideUserDao(db: AppDatabase): UserDao = db.userDao() } // :core:data — binds repository interface to implementation @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) abstract class DataModule { @Binds @Singleton abstract fun bindUserRepository( impl: UserRepositoryImpl ): UserRepository } // :feature:home — feature-specific bindings (if needed) @Module @InstallIn(ViewModelComponent::class) object HomeModule { @Provides @ViewModelScoped fun provideHomeAnalytics(): HomeAnalytics = HomeAnalytics() }
How it works: Each module is compiled independently with its @Module classes. The Hilt Gradle plugin aggregates all modules at the :app level, building the complete dependency graph. If any binding is missing, you get a compile error in the :app module's build.

@EntryPoint — Accessing Hilt from Non-Hilt Modules

Some modules can't use Hilt directly (dynamic feature modules, library modules without Hilt plugin). Use @EntryPoint to bridge the gap.

// In :core:data or :app — define what's accessible @EntryPoint @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) interface DataEntryPoint { fun userRepository(): UserRepository fun analyticsService(): AnalyticsService } // In a dynamic feature module (no Hilt plugin): class DynamicFeatureActivity : AppCompatActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) val entryPoint = EntryPointAccessors.fromApplication( applicationContext, DataEntryPoint::class.java ) val repository = entryPoint.userRepository() val analytics = entryPoint.analyticsService() } } // In a ContentProvider (initialized before Application): class MyContentProvider : ContentProvider() { override fun onCreate(): Boolean { val entryPoint = EntryPointAccessors.fromApplication( context!!.applicationContext, DataEntryPoint::class.java ) entryPoint.analyticsService().trackAppStart() return true } }

Navigation Between Feature Modules

Features can't depend on each other, so how do you navigate from :feature:home to :feature:profile? The answer: shared navigation routes in a :core:navigation module and lambda callbacks.

// ======================================== // :core:navigation/Routes.kt // ======================================== // ALL feature modules depend on this — it defines the routes sealed class Route(val route: String) { data object Home : Route("home") data class Profile(val userId: String) : Route("profile/{userId}") { fun createRoute(userId: String) = "profile/$userId" } data object Settings : Route("settings") } // ======================================== // :feature:home/HomeScreen.kt // ======================================== // Home exposes a navigation callback — it does NOT navigate itself @Composable fun HomeScreen( viewModel: HomeViewModel = hiltViewModel(), onNavigateToProfile: (userId: String) -> Unit, onNavigateToSettings: () -> Unit ) { val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() LazyColumn { items(uiState.users) { user -> UserCard( user = user, onClick = { onNavigateToProfile(user.id) } ) } } } // ======================================== // :app/navigation/AppNavHost.kt // ======================================== // :app depends on ALL features — it wires navigation together @Composable fun AppNavHost(navController: NavHostController) { NavHost(navController, startDestination = Route.Home.route) { composable(Route.Home.route) { HomeScreen( onNavigateToProfile = { userId -> navController.navigate(Route.Profile.createRoute(userId)) }, onNavigateToSettings = { navController.navigate(Route.Settings.route) } ) } composable("profile/{userId}") { backStackEntry -> ProfileScreen( userId = backStackEntry.arguments?.getString("userId") ?: "" ) } composable(Route.Settings.route) { SettingsScreen() } } }
Navigation Flow Between Feature Modules: :feature:home :feature:profile +-------------------+ +-------------------+ | HomeScreen | | ProfileScreen | | | | | | onNavigateToProfile: (String) -> Unit | | | | | | +-------|-----------+ +-------------------+ | ^ | :app/AppNavHost.kt | | +---------------------------+ | +---> | navController.navigate( |--+ | Route.Profile.create() | +---------------------------+ Depends on: :feature:home, :feature:profile, :core:navigation Key: HomeScreen has NO import from :feature:profile The :app module is the only place that knows about both features

KSP vs KAPT

KAPTKSP
Uses Java annotation processing via stubsKotlin-native symbol processing
Generates Java stubs first, then runs processing — slowOperates on Kotlin directly — up to 2x faster
Legacy: Room, Dagger/Hilt (migrating)Room, Moshi, and most modern libraries
// Migration: replace kapt with ksp plugins { id("com.google.devtools.ksp") version "2.0.0-1.0.22" } dependencies { ksp(libs.room.compiler) // Fast // kapt(libs.room.compiler) // Slow — remove this }

Build Performance

  • Configuration cache — Caches the build configuration phase: org.gradle.configuration-cache=true
  • Parallel execution — Build independent modules simultaneously: org.gradle.parallel=true
  • Build cache — Reuse outputs from previous builds: org.gradle.caching=true
# gradle.properties — recommended settings for multi-module projects # Parallel compilation (huge win with many modules) org.gradle.parallel=true # Build cache — reuse outputs when inputs haven't changed org.gradle.caching=true # Configuration cache — cache the configuration phase org.gradle.configuration-cache=true # Increase Gradle daemon memory for large projects org.gradle.jvmargs=-Xmx4g -XX:+UseParallelGC # Use non-transitive R classes (smaller R.java, faster builds) android.nonTransitiveRClass=true # Use non-final resource IDs (required for library modules) android.nonFinalResIds=true
Build speed impact of modularization: A monolithic app recompiles everything on any change. A modularized app with 20 modules can compile 15 unchanged modules from cache and only recompile the 5 affected ones — in parallel. Real-world impact: 5-minute builds become 30-second builds.

R8 Full Mode vs Compat Mode

R8 full mode enables more aggressive optimizations:

// gradle.properties android.enableR8.fullMode=true

Full mode: removes more unused code, may break if you rely on implicit keep rules (e.g., reflection). Always test after enabling. Compat mode is the safe default that follows ProGuard behavior.

Putting It All Together — Project Structure

// Complete multi-module project structure my-android-app/ +-- build-logic/ | +-- convention/ | +-- src/main/kotlin/ | +-- AndroidApplicationConventionPlugin.kt | +-- AndroidLibraryConventionPlugin.kt | +-- AndroidFeatureConventionPlugin.kt | +-- AndroidHiltConventionPlugin.kt | +-- AndroidComposeConventionPlugin.kt | +-- app/ | +-- build.gradle.kts | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/app/ | +-- MyApplication.kt // @HiltAndroidApp | +-- MainActivity.kt // @AndroidEntryPoint, NavHost | +-- navigation/ | +-- AppNavHost.kt // wires all feature screens | +-- feature/ | +-- home/ | | +-- build.gradle.kts | | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/feature/home/ | | +-- HomeViewModel.kt | | +-- HomeScreen.kt | | +-- HomeUiState.kt | +-- profile/ | | +-- build.gradle.kts | | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/feature/profile/ | | +-- ProfileViewModel.kt | | +-- ProfileScreen.kt | | +-- ProfileUiState.kt | +-- settings/ | +-- build.gradle.kts | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/feature/settings/ | +-- SettingsViewModel.kt | +-- SettingsScreen.kt | +-- core/ | +-- data/ | | +-- build.gradle.kts | | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/core/data/ | | +-- di/DataModule.kt // @Binds repo implementations | | +-- repository/ | | | +-- UserRepositoryImpl.kt | | | +-- SettingsRepositoryImpl.kt | | +-- mapper/ | | +-- UserMapper.kt // DTO/Entity -> Domain model | +-- domain/ | | +-- build.gradle.kts // pure Kotlin JVM module | | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/core/domain/ | | +-- model/ | | | +-- User.kt | | +-- repository/ | | | +-- UserRepository.kt // interface | | +-- usecase/ | | +-- GetUserProfileUseCase.kt | +-- network/ | | +-- build.gradle.kts | | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/core/network/ | | +-- di/NetworkModule.kt | | +-- api/UserApi.kt | | +-- dto/UserDto.kt | +-- database/ | | +-- build.gradle.kts | | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/core/database/ | | +-- di/DatabaseModule.kt | | +-- AppDatabase.kt | | +-- dao/UserDao.kt | | +-- entity/UserEntity.kt | +-- ui/ | | +-- build.gradle.kts | | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/core/ui/ | | +-- theme/Theme.kt | | +-- components/AppTopBar.kt | | +-- components/LoadingIndicator.kt | +-- common/ | | +-- build.gradle.kts | | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/core/common/ | | +-- Result.kt | | +-- Extensions.kt | +-- navigation/ | | +-- build.gradle.kts | | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/core/navigation/ | | +-- Routes.kt | +-- testing/ | +-- build.gradle.kts | +-- src/main/kotlin/com/example/core/testing/ | +-- FakeUserRepository.kt | +-- TestDispatcherRule.kt | +-- gradle/ | +-- libs.versions.toml // version catalog +-- settings.gradle.kts // includes all modules +-- gradle.properties // build performance flags
// settings.gradle.kts — include all modules rootProject.name = "my-android-app" includeBuild("build-logic") include( ":app", ":feature:home", ":feature:profile", ":feature:settings", ":core:data", ":core:domain", ":core:network", ":core:database", ":core:ui", ":core:common", ":core:navigation", ":core:testing" )

questions.title: Module Dependency Rules
questions.description: In a properly modularized Android app following the Now in Android (NIA) architecture, which dependency rule is correct?
options: Feature modules can depend on other feature modules for code sharing
Feature modules depend only on :core:* modules, never on each other
Core modules can depend on feature modules for UI components
:app depends only on :core:data, features register themselves
questions.explanation: Feature modules must NEVER depend on each other — this would create tight coupling and slow builds. Features depend only on :core:* modules. The :app module depends on all features to assemble them. Core modules never depend on features. This DAG (directed acyclic graph) enables parallel compilation.
hints: What would happen to build parallelism if :feature:home depended on :feature:profile?

questions.title: api vs implementation
questions.description: If :core:data uses implementation(libs.retrofit), what happens when a :feature:home module depends on :core:data?
options: :feature:home can use Retrofit classes directly
:feature:home cannot see Retrofit classes — they are internal to :core:data
The build fails because Retrofit is not available
:feature:home gets Retrofit but only at runtime, not compile time
questions.explanation: implementation means the dependency is internal. :feature:home can use :core:data's public API but cannot access Retrofit types. This is encapsulation — if :core:data switches from Retrofit to Ktor, no feature modules need to change. Use api only when your module's public interface exposes types from the dependency.
hints: implementation = private, api = public in terms of transitive visibility

questions.title: Version Catalog Structure
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to complete this version catalog entry in libs.versions.toml.
questions.explanation: Version catalogs have four sections: [versions] (version numbers), [libraries] (Maven coordinates), [bundles] (groups of libraries), and [plugins] (Gradle plugins). version.ref links a library to a version, ensuring consistency.
hints: The TOML file has sections in square brackets
version.ref points to a key in the versions section

questions.title: Convention Plugin
questions.description: What does this convention plugin achieve?
options: It creates a new Android feature module from scratch at runtime
It applies a standard set of plugins, SDK versions, Compose support, and shared dependencies so every feature module has consistent configuration with one line
It enforces that feature modules cannot depend on each other by analyzing the dependency graph
It generates build variants for debug and release configurations
questions.explanation: Convention plugins centralize build configuration. Instead of repeating compileSdk, minSdk, Compose setup, Hilt, and common dependencies in every build.gradle.kts, this plugin applies them all with id("myapp.android.feature"). This is the build-logic/ pattern used by Now in Android — it reduces duplication and ensures consistency across all feature modules.
hints: Think DRY principle applied to build configuration
The Now in Android project uses this pattern in build-logic/

questions.title: KSP vs KAPT
questions.description: Why is KSP (Kotlin Symbol Processing) faster than KAPT?
options: KSP generates less code than KAPT
KSP operates directly on Kotlin symbols without generating intermediate Java stubs
KSP runs at runtime instead of compile time
KSP uses incremental processing while KAPT processes everything from scratch
questions.explanation: KAPT first generates Java stubs from Kotlin code, then runs Java annotation processing on those stubs — a costly extra step. KSP is Kotlin-native and processes Kotlin symbols directly, skipping stub generation entirely. This makes it up to 2x faster. Most modern libraries (Room, Moshi) now support KSP.
hints: The bottleneck in KAPT is an intermediate conversion step

questions.title: Leaking Internal Dependencies
questions.description: This module's build configuration leaks implementation details. Feature modules are now tightly coupled to Retrofit. Fix it.
questions.explanation: Retrofit, Moshi converter, and OkHttp logging are implementation details of :core:network. Feature modules should call repository.getUsers() — they don't need to know it uses Retrofit underneath. Only :core:model should be api because data classes from it appear in the module's public function signatures.
hints: Ask: do feature modules need to directly use Retrofit classes?
Which dependency's types appear in the module's public API?

questions.title: Inter-Module Navigation
questions.description: Feature modules can't depend on each other, so how do you navigate from :feature:home to :feature:profile? Write a shared navigation route definition in :core:navigation and the navigation graph setup.

checks.message: Define type-safe routes in the shared module
Show navigation using the shared routes

questions.explanation: The pattern: (1) Define routes in :core:navigation which all modules can access. (2) Feature screens expose navigation events as lambda callbacks (e.g., onNavigateToProfile). (3) The :app module wires everything together in NavHost, since it depends on all features. This way :feature:home never imports anything from :feature:profile.
hints: The :app module is the only one that depends on all feature modules
Feature screens should expose navigation events as callbacks, not perform navigation directly

questions.title: Build Performance
questions.description: Which Gradle property enables reusing task outputs from previous builds, significantly speeding up incremental builds?
options: org.gradle.parallel=true
org.gradle.caching=true
org.gradle.configuration-cache=true
org.gradle.daemon=true
questions.explanation: org.gradle.caching=true enables the build cache, which stores and reuses task outputs. If inputs haven't changed, the cached output is used instead of re-running the task. parallel runs independent modules simultaneously. configuration-cache caches the build configuration phase. All three should be enabled for optimal build speed.
hints: Which setting is specifically about reusing previous outputs?

questions.title: R8 Full Mode
questions.description: What is the key difference between R8 full mode and R8 compat mode?
options: Full mode only works on Kotlin code, compat mode handles Java
Full mode does not obfuscate, compat mode does
Full mode applies more aggressive optimizations and may remove classes kept implicitly by ProGuard rules — requires explicit keep rules for reflection
Full mode produces larger APKs with better runtime performance
questions.explanation: R8 compat mode follows ProGuard semantics — it keeps classes that ProGuard would keep implicitly. Full mode is more aggressive: it may remove classes only accessed via reflection unless you add explicit -keep rules. This produces smaller APKs but requires testing. Enable with android.enableR8.fullMode=true.
hints: Full mode breaks implicit assumptions about which classes are kept

questions.title: Build Variants
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define product flavors for a free and paid version of the app.

blanks.hint: The block that defines app variants
Unique identifier for each variant on the Play Store

questions.explanation: productFlavors create variants of your app with different configurations. Each flavor can have its own applicationId (allowing both free and paid versions on the same device), buildConfigField values, resources, and source sets. Combined with build types (debug/release), they produce build variants like freeDebug, paidRelease.
hints: Product flavors let you build multiple versions of the same app
Each variant needs a unique package identifier to coexist on a device

questions.title: Module Dependency Direction
questions.description: In a multi-module project, :core:ui needs to display a User data class defined in :core:domain. Meanwhile, :core:data implements the UserRepository interface defined in :core:domain. Which dependency setup is correct?
options: :core:domain depends on :core:data and :core:ui — domain is the central hub
:core:data depends on :core:domain (to implement interfaces), :core:ui depends on :core:domain (to use models), :core:domain depends on nothing
:core:ui depends on :core:data which depends on :core:domain — a linear chain
All three depend on each other — they're tightly coupled by nature
questions.explanation: :core:domain is the innermost layer — it defines models and interfaces but depends on nothing. :core:data depends on :core:domain to implement repository interfaces. :core:ui depends on :core:domain to display domain models. This follows the Dependency Inversion Principle: high-level modules (domain) don't depend on low-level modules (data). Instead, data depends on domain via interfaces.
hints: Which module should have ZERO dependencies on other project modules?
Think about the Dependency Inversion Principle — which way do dependencies flow?

questions.title: Hilt @EntryPoint in Multi-Module
questions.description: A dynamic feature module (:feature:premium) is delivered on-demand via Play Feature Delivery and does NOT have the Hilt Gradle plugin. What does this code accomplish?
options: It installs Hilt into the dynamic feature module at runtime
It creates a separate dependency graph for the premium module
It provides a bridge for the dynamic feature module to access Hilt-managed dependencies from the app's dependency graph without requiring the Hilt plugin
It replaces the UserRepository and BillingService bindings for premium users
questions.explanation: Dynamic feature modules are compiled separately and may not have access to the Hilt Gradle plugin. @EntryPoint defines an interface that exposes specific dependencies from the app's Hilt graph. The dynamic feature module uses EntryPointAccessors to reach into the existing graph and get the dependencies it needs. This is the standard pattern for Hilt + Play Feature Delivery.
hints: Dynamic feature modules can't use @AndroidEntryPoint — they need another way to access dependencies
The @EntryPoint interface acts as a controlled window into the Hilt graph

questions.title: Convention Plugin — Missing Compose
questions.description: A developer created a new feature module using the convention plugin, but Compose functions won't compile. The error says @Composable invocations can only happen from the context of a @Composable function. The convention plugin is missing something. Fix it.

checks.message: Enable Compose in the build features

questions.explanation: The convention plugin was missing buildFeatures.compose = true in the LibraryExtension configuration, and the Kotlin Compose compiler plugin (org.jetbrains.kotlin.plugin.compose). Without these, the Kotlin compiler doesn't process @Composable annotations. This is a common pitfall when setting up convention plugins — every feature module needs Compose enabled if it has UI.
hints: What Gradle configuration enables the Compose compiler?
The LibraryExtension has a buildFeatures block — what's missing from it?

questions.title: Pure Kotlin Module Benefits
questions.description: Why should :core:domain (containing domain models and repository interfaces) be a pure Kotlin JVM module (org.jetbrains.kotlin.jvm) instead of an Android library (com.android.library)?
options: Android library modules can't contain interfaces or data classes

Pure Kotlin modules compile faster (no Android resource processing, no AAPT, no manifest merging), enforce no Android framework dependencies in your domain layer, and can be shared with non-Android targets
Pure Kotlin modules support coroutines while Android library modules don't
There's no practical difference — it's just a naming convention
questions.explanation: A pure Kotlin JVM module skips all Android build steps (AAPT, resource processing, manifest merging, dexing). This makes it the fastest module type to compile. It also enforces Clean Architecture principles: if you accidentally import an Android class in your domain model, the build fails immediately. Finally, pure Kotlin modules can be shared with KMP (Kotlin Multiplatform) targets like iOS or desktop.
hints: Think about what build steps are unique to Android library modules
What happens if someone accidentally imports android.content.Context in a domain model?
